name: Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia
body: As I was growing up, I remember my father being a fan of Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia. We had a few of his studio albums that were played regularly at home. Needless to say, I became a fan as well. Being quite fond of Qawali at that age, I also recall that the voice of Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia bore some resemblance to the legendary duo of Shankar Shambu. For many reasons, Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia remained a favourite among many family members and friends.

Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia was born in the village of Nangal Shahu located in the district of Sialkot, Pakistan in 1936. His father’s name was Bhai Dharam Singh Ji and mother was Mata Dhan Kaur Ji. Due to the turmoil in 1947 as a result of the partition, their family moved to the village of Choti Miani in Kapurthala district where their land was allotted.

At this time, the renowned Kirtani, Bhai Gyan Singh Jogi was also living in Choti Miani and Rasia Ji began to learn Kirtan from him. Due to some financial difficulties, Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia started to do Kirtan at around the age of 16 or 17. Initially, he was in a Dhadhi jatha and played the ‘dhad’, sarangi and a few other instruments. When he was around 18 years old, Bhai Gyan Singh Jogi started a Kirtani jatha and Rasia Ji accompanied him on the side harmonium. They remained together for about 7 years, after which Bhai Gyan Singh Jogi advised him to form his own jatha.

He was around 25 years old when he formed his own jatha with his younger brother, Bhai Charan Singh Ji accompanying him. They came to Delhi in 1965 and served at Gurdwara Sahib Moti Nagar for 7 years. In 1972, he applied for a position of Hazoori Raagi under the employment of the Delhi Sikh Gurdwara Management Committee (DSGMC). There were 102 applications but only 2 positions were available. Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia and his jatha managed to secure one of the positions and served as a Hazoori Raagi in Gurdwara Sees Ganj Sahib and Gurdwara Bangla Sahib for many years. Their popularity skyrocketed over a short period of time and they were invited for Kirtan Darbars across India and also for tours abroad. They also released around 35 Kirtan albums which became very popular.

In 1982, Bhai Charan Singh Ji met in an unfortunate accident and passed away. Following the accident, Rasia Ji’s son, Bhai Ravinder Singh Rasia joined him on the side harmonium. Bhai Ravinder Singh Rasia was trained in Indian classical music for almost 10 years at the Delhi Sangeet Vidhyala. While they were in Delhi, Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia also learned to play the mandolin under guidance of Sardar Jaswant Singh Ji, who was an artist at the Delhi radio station. With rigorous practice, Rasia Ji became very adept at playing the mandolin. He was also featured playing the mandolin in 2 records released by His Master’s Voice (HMV) recording company.

In 1987, Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia received an invitation to perform Kirtan in New York, United States (US). His youngest brother, Bhai Amrik Singh accompanied them on ‘tabla’ during this tour. Their Kirtan was very well received by the Sangat of Richmond Hill Gurdwara. As a result, they stayed in Richmond Hill for 6 months before travelling to other parts of the US. When they came to Los Angeles, the Hollywood Sikh Gurdwara requested them to stay on as Head Kirtani and Head Granthi at the Gurdwara. At that time, the situation in India was not ideal in the aftermath of Operation Bluestar, so they decided to stay on in Los Angeles. Bhai Sohan Singh Ji served in the Hollywood Sikh Gurdwara for 12 years.

One of the main pillars of support in Rasia Ji’s life was his wife, Bibi Sumitar Kaur Ji. She took an active role in getting him ready to form his own jatha on Bhai Gyan Singh Jogi’s advice. She travelled to Amritsar and brought back many Gurbani ‘Pothis’ for him. She would sit with him and recite Shabads to help him memorize them. At that time, there was an empty mansion opposite their home where they tied their livestock. Bibi Sumitar Kaur Ji would encourage him to go there daily and practice alone. She told him to imagine that he was surrounded by Sangat and they were listening attentively to every Shabad that he sang and every word he spoke. Both of them were blessed with five children; two sons and three daughters. Their eldest son, Bhai Ravinder Singh Rasia currently does Kirtan in the form of service along with his younger brother, Bhai Parvinder Singh Rasia, who is an exceptional tabla player. Their daughters’ names are Sukhjit Kaur, Ravinder Kaur and Baljit Kaur. Completing his journey in this world, Bhai Sohan Singh Ji Rasia took his last breath on 11th November 2016. The information above was obtained from Bhai Ravinder Singh Rasia.
If anyone has more recordings or photos of Bhai Sohan Singh Rasia to share, please contact us.
